title: Roll Cage Question (300zx)
text:
Q: How do I fit a legal roll cage in my 300ZX when I'm 6'4" and my head is too close to the roof?
A: You have several options: drop the floor pan (popular on Miatas), lean the seat back, use bucket seats instead of factory seats, or a combination of these approaches. Mount your seat first and have your tallest driver sit in it with a helmet on to measure clearance. You can also contact John Pagel, the Lemons Head Tech, with pictures for guidance on cage design. The cage must pass the "broomstick" test, which checks that it fits within 2 inches inside the exterior dimensions of the cage volume. While technically you could extend the cage outside the roof, this risks head contact with the factory roof during a rollover.
author: ClaudeSmoote87
original_text_truncated:
Hello all, I'm new here. My dumb buddies and I decided that this is a great way to spend money and have fun. We bought an 84 Nissan 300zx non t-top, and here's the issue. I'm tall (6'4") and even sitting on the floor of the car my head is kind of close to the roof. Does anyone else have experience with putting a legal cage in one of these? Should we chop and raise the roof? Or just try to put the cage right against it and just lean the seat back really far? Also what are the things we should check to not break two laps in? Any advice is appreciated.

Look into "drop the floor pan" popular with Miatas. Mount the seat before planning the cage, and seat your tallest (sitting) driver with helmet on to measure. Read this constantly, and understand the "broomstick" test (it's 2" inside the volume defined by the exterior dimensions of the cage) : How to Not Fail Lemons Tech Inspection Yes, you can do an 'exocage'; also a center bar in the halo section. Contact John Pagel (Pagel@24hoursoflemons.com), Lemons Head Tech, with pictures and questions. And read the "stickies" in the forum subtopics, and their responses. It's both entertaining and amazingly informative.

You can drop the seat as mentioned earlier, or lean it back, but you may need a combination of everything if your head is hitting the roof. Is your head hitting the roof with factory seats, or with bucket seats? Your seat selection can make a big difference here. I'm not the biggest fan of it, but technically you would be within rules to extend the cage outside of/through the roof to meet the helmet to roll cage requirements. There's a couple miatas that do this through their hardtop, but I'm not the biggest fan of it because you are still at risk of your head contacting the factory roof during a rollover which might be enough to cause a neck injury.

citation: Forum: Nearing 2 years since we built the car...so I have
title: Nearing 2 years since we built the car...so I have questions.
text:
Q: How do we get our fire suppression bottle serviced and what's the helmet recertification timeline for 2026?
A: Fire suppression bottles can be shipped for servicing—Discovery Parts in Georgia accepts them via UPS ground shipping with about a week and a half turnaround. Snell 2020 helmets are good for 10 years from manufacture, so your 2020 helmets will be valid through 2030. However, Lemons will allow older SA2015 helmets through the end of 2026, so you have some flexibility if you're running those instead.
author: The Stuart
original_text_truncated:
Hola fellow lunatics. We are rapidly approaching 2 years since we built our car and need to get some things sorted before our next race at Barber. We have a SPA technique fire suppression bottle, has anyone found a way to get it properly serviced/recertified without taking it to a service center? The nearest one to us is around 3 and a half hours away. I've seen that some people ship them but also say they get frequently turned down when attempting to ship it. Snell2020 helmets - I thought i remember that helmets were good for 10 years but we were told at NCM that our helmets will need to be replaced for 2026. Is it 5 years or 10 years? Thanks for any help ya'll have!

Not sure about the helmets. I'd check the rules. As for the bottle, we send ours out, never had an issue.

Stuart, I've had no issues shipping bottles to Discovery Parts in Georgia. Their website has instructions, and be sure to use UPS ground shipping. Plan on a week and a half or so turnaround time. Your 2020 helmets are good for 10 years (at least for Lemons), and usually 6 to 12 months additionally since the new SA certifications often don't come out until October or November. I asked at NCM tech inspection specifically about SA2015 helmets and was told by the tech inspector that Lemons will allow SA2015 helmets through the end of 2026.

citation: Forum: How do I make a N/A Subaru EJ25 last?
title: How do I make a N/A Subaru EJ25 last?
text:
Q: What are the key reliability issues with a naturally aspirated EJ25 Subaru engine for Lemons racing, and how can I prevent them?
A: The main concerns are oiling problems during hard cornering and oil burning. Use an OEM MLS head gasket and consider swapping in a 2006+ STI oil pan and pickup system, which handles cornering better. Run thicker oil (5W-40 or heavier) to reduce burning. Also proactively replace wheel bearings before racing, inspect the rear subframe for rust, and consider an accusump system if you're concerned about oil starvation in the heads during sustained cornering. Keep the engine stock otherwise—teams that modify Subarus for speed tend to have reliability issues.
author: racerxr
original_text_truncated:
It's been a minute since I last posted and raced. Just checked, it's been 10 years. I was just gifted a very stock 250,000 mile 25 yr old Subaru Forester wanting to go to Valhalla. I think it's time I get back to racing Lemons. I assume there are a few Subaru teams with some wisdom to share. So, how do I go about making a normally aspirated EJ251 last a full weekend or longer? From a little internet searching, sounds like OEM MLS head gaskets are kinda important. Anything else problematic like oil pan pickup/baffling or oil return from those horizontal heads? Any weak points in the drivetrain (MT5 trans with R160 diff)? BTW, I raced a '93 Mazda 626 for ~13 races in Lemons and another series about 10-14 years ago. Kept the motor stock and spent my time making factory suspension and brake parts work better. I plan to do more of the same with this car. Not looking for cheaty speedy engine updates. Not going to turbo charge this motor. I just want to make it nearly as bullet proof as that Mazda FSDE 2.0L was. Is that possible? I've been impressed with this EJ251 on the street. It's super torquey, even more so than that long stroke Mazda FSDE motor. That's a very good trait for a Lemons racer. Suspension and brake updates are easy too with local junkyards full of later model Subarus with bigger brakes, bigger sway bars, shorter stiffer struts/springs, and the brackets that should bolt up to this old beast. My goal with this car is to keep the motor original with the emissions equipment intact. I'll even keep much of the dash, heater core, and blower. I'd like to keep it registered. Looks like Colorado only requires an emissions test for registration. So, ditching airbags, original seat belts, etc won't keep it from being registered and driven to the track.

If someone is racing a Mazda FSDE 2.0L powered 626, Probe, or Protege. I'll be happy to share my hard earned wisdom.

We race a 96 impreza with a 2.5 swap from an 05 outback. Oiling is an issue. Depending on how hard you can corner (what suspension and tire you run) 06+ ej gets the sti oil pan and pickup which is much better for cornering oil problems. Oil burning is also an issue, solutions include thicker oil (5w40 or more) and or engine rebuild (new rings) There are other fancier solutions that are expensive and I haven't tried. Wheel bearings car also be common, best to replace pre race

"How do I make a N/A Subaru EJ25 last?" Remove it from the car and wrap it in foil? Empirical evidence with subarus in Lemons seems to be the slower you are, the longer it lasts. Only one or two teams has managed to achieve top spots with a subaru, and I've never seen them sharing their secrets. Zacks is right about wheel bearings, don't assume the engine is the only issue (seems that depending on your area of the country, rear shocks punching holes through rusty support panels also happens with an alarming degree of frequency).

Idk about other regions but seems like the subaru secret to success is mod it to be fast, then 50/50 if you do well or blow up each race

I knew about the wheel bearing thing. I'll take advantage of all of the exempt safety items. I eventually did that on my Mazda 626 and had more fun. I do enjoy wrenching on the car. But, I prefer to do it in my driveway instead of at the track to the sounds of everyone else having fun on the track. Wrestling with a set of Subaru rear wheel bearings track side is a level of hell I prefer to avoid. Good point regarding the rusty state of old cars. This one has lived it's life in the Colorado mountains. Fortunately, the rear strut towers and all mount points on the unibody look good. But, the rear subframe is looking a bit rough. So, I'll be checking the pick and pull yards for one that is not so dangerous.

The usual rust belt procedure here in Ohio is "hit it hard with a hammer and if holes appear, fix it".

The tip regarding the 06+ oil pan and pickup is exactly the type of tips I was hoping to learn. I can definitely make that on

title: Mounting anti-submarine belts
text:
Q: How should I mount anti-submarine belts on my race car, and what forces do they actually experience in a crash?
A: Anti-submarine belts should mount behind the driver's position, ideally directly under or slightly back from the crotch area, at roughly a 20-degree angle rearward. They prevent the driver from sliding forward under the lap belts during a crash and experience relatively little force compared to lap and shoulder harnesses. Mount them to reinforced floor points using large washers and bolts (torqued to manufacturer spec), and avoid sharp corners on spreader plates that belts might contact. The belts should have minimal slack when properly adjusted—if your lap belts pull up toward your belly button, your anti-submarine belt mounting angle is likely wrong.
author: lemonracingnewb
original_text_truncated:
Hey everyone, I have my seat + seat-frame all figured out, mounting directly to the factory mount points of my 96 Mustang. I have a 6pt harness I'm using and I'm trying to figure out how to mount the 2 anti-submarine belts. Here is a top-down view of the floorpan. I've circled where the factory seat mount points are located and I've also circled where I plan on mounting the anti-submarine belts. In this location the sub belts will exit the hole cut-out in the seat pan at a 20degree angle which I believe is ideal. I've also included a side-view. You can see the seat is 3.25" off the ground so I've decided to mount the bolts 1.2" behind the seat cutout which should give me my 20 degree angle. A few questions: 1\ From the top-down view you can see I'd have to mount the submarine belts to the floorpan at a point where its at a slight angle. Is that ok? 2\ Is it ok that I'm gonna mount these belts so close to the rear mount points of the factor seat? 3\ The underside of the floorpan is not flat. My spreader plat is a 3"x3" piece of 1/4" steel. It wont form to the floor pan directly. Is that ok? Thanks in advnace.

The main thing with the anti-sub belts is that they are behind the driver's position, ideally back under the spine of whoever is sitting the most rearward. This was the direction I was given by TEO at Sonoma this year. To the rest, I dunno. Everything seems reasonble to me. Be sure there are no sharp corners on your spreader/floor reinforcement.

No sharp corners? My spreader plates are just 3"x3" square plate. Are you suggesting I round the corners off a bit? I guess that would be so its less easy for it to tear the the sheet metal if things get deformed during crash?

Are you sure that's 20° back? Those proposed mounting points seem really far back. Depending on the belt manufacturer, the mounting point seems to vary. I've seen ones where it's 20° rearward off the chest plane; since most seats have a 20° layback angle, this basically results in the mounting point being straight down from your crotch. I've also seen 0-20° back from crotch. The way I interpret it is straight down from crotch, or slightly back, but never forward. 1) The mounting plates you are showing are supposed to be mounting in the same direction of tension on the webbing. The downside of that mounting style is it rarely works out this way. I'm 99% sure it'll be fine since this should self-correct itself in an accident if it isn't too far off; my first 5 years of doing this stuff I used that style of harness mounts, but I've since switched over to only eyelets and clip-in harnesses. 2) As long as the mount is reinforced with welded in plates or backup plates, the angles are correct, and the webbing isn't obstructed, it doesn't matter where you mount them if all of those are satisfied. 3) I'm shocked that you aren't forming the floor pan as you tighten it up. With 1/4" plate, you should absolutely sandwhich your floor pan as you tighten it. The 7/16-20 eye bolts say 30ftlbs in the manual, but the torque spec for a 7/16-20 bolt is around 70-90 ft-lbs. I personally go with the latter as it helps sandwhich whatever I'm bolting it to, and it lets the bolt stay in tension during an accident rather than switch over to shear (there is over 10,000 lbs of tension in that bolt at 80ftlbs.. which is greater than what that eye bolt will see in an accident, and doesn't make sense why your not able to flatten your floor pan).

Thanks for the very well thought out replies. I appreciate it. I'll respond to your points/questions here. I am confident it is 20 degrees back. that green 'X' in the top down view is where essentially directly beneath my crouch. The seat sits 3.25" off the ground so putting the mounting points 1.2" behind the green 'X' ensures an ~20degree angle. Ignoring angles for a moment, the mount points are essentially directly beneath my butt when I sit in the seat so I believe I am OK but if you have a concern of course please speak up. I hadn't consid

title: DIY car loading ramps
text:
Q: What are the best ways to build or buy ramps to load a race car into a box truck or similar tall hauler?
A: Teams use several approaches: buy commercial aluminum ramps rated for 7000+ lbs (available from discount ramp suppliers or Amazon), use angle iron like trailers do, or build DIY wooden ramps from thick 2x10 boards. To prevent bumper scraping on the steep angle, add extension pieces at the ramp entrance, use blocks to break up the angle, or lower the truck bed by driving the truck itself onto ramps first. Some teams also use hinged ramp sections that can adjust to weight distribution, and a winch makes loading much easier.
author: Klayfish
original_text_truncated:
I can't get the damn picture to post, but I bought a retired 26' Uhaul box truck that had the box cut off but the load floor remains. Going to use it as a car hauler and RV tow pig. I've seen a number of Lemons teams use buses, box trucks etc to haul their car. How do you make ramps to load your car? Obviously it can't be too steep of an angle so the car doesn't scrape or get high centered. The load height is 2'11"

most trailers just use 2x2x0.25 angle iron for ramps. They'd be a bit heavy for the length you would need. https://thetrailerpartsoutlet.com/produ ... PMQAvD_BwE

Buy a good winch too. Not impossible to make a standard rear door that folds down like a trailer. Also look at ramps people use to load ATVs into pickup trucks, just as bad height differential and might inspire you. You can also continue the ramp inside the box. See something like this: https://www.hardlinecrawlers.com/attach ... eg.113437/

I understand your diy question; personally, I'm hoping HF gets in their newer stronger 3,000 lb. (each@) ramps soon. They've advertised it in their displays for years.

I'm guessing you already thought of this idea but figured I'd mention it anyways.. you can also carry some car ramps and drive the front of the box truck onto the ramps to lower the rear/get your angle started. We do the same concept on our trailer by dropping the tongue jack.

You might look up Alex Vendler (GnomeFabtech (sp) on the forum). The MetroGnome was transported in a box truck. IIRC, they had a bunch of ramp sections with stands along the way and winched it up.

Discountramps.com. Has what you need. Aluminum to any length out to 14’. I have a set that I used to load a car into a 26’ U-Haul and it worked great. Rated for 7000 and weight 112 for a pair of 14’ long ramps.

Man, I sure don't see anything on that website anywhere near that price point and description. Was that purchased several years ago before recent price increases? Could you supply a link to what you purchased? Oh, and stupid me - I used Chrome to look on the website instead of my usual DuckDuckGo, so now every ad I see is for their website - very intrusive, and a lot more thorough ad penetration than most other web sites. Be careful!

Recommended reading. https://24hoursoflemons.com/blog/race-c ... truck-2-0/

Brilliant! And, has PICS! (How come I missed that? I've been reading this site since 2015, and _still_ missed that...)

Man, I sure don't see anything on that website anywhere near that price point and description. Was that purchased several years ago before recent price increases? Could you supply a link to what you purchased? Oh, and stupid me - I used Chrome to look on the website instead of my usual DuckDuckGo, so now every ad I see is for their website - very intrusive, and a lot more thorough ad penetration than most other web sites. Be careful! Same ramps on Amazon for 675 per ramp.

Ah, I see my mistake now: I saw the '112' as the price, not the weight. (I'm going to blame it on having COVID)

Check with Anton Lovett. He transports the Volvo in a bread truck and it isn’t a lowered platform model.

Cool idea from U-Haul, lots of teams use that. We used to make our own ramps out of thick 2x10 boards about 10 feet long, and we also added "extensions" at an angle, because without them the bumper would really scrape. Some guys just put blocks in front of the ramps to break up the angle. Another cool thing is to make a hinge so the ramps can "walk" a little and adjust to the weight.

title: Lighting "hard cutoff" for 24hr
text:
Q: Do our factory headlights need to have a sharp cutoff line at 40 inches, or is some light scatter acceptable?
A: Factory halogen headlights in their original location with proper aiming are fine even if they don't have a perfectly sharp cutoff—judges understand older lights naturally scatter some light. The hard cutoff rule (40 inches at 25 feet) is primarily enforced against projector-based and aftermarket LED lights. For overnight races, your lights will be tested against a board with a marked line the evening before; if you pass, you get a sticker allowing you to run during headlight hours. Even with a passing sticker, cars can still be black-flagged during the race if lighting becomes an issue.
author: Zacks
original_text_truncated:
Probably answered before, tried search but no luck. Rules say iirc lights must have a hard cutoff of 40in or less at 25ft. We where testing ours last night and our OEM lights with REGULAR BULBS don't have really any cutoff, they are shitty headlights from 96. The bulbs sit 22in off ground and the main brightness stopps around 18in or so at 25 ft BUT there is still some dimmer light, well everywhere... So I would say the headlights are aimed correctly per factory but do not have a "hard cutoff" I figure factory headlights in not obnoxious settings will be ok, but I'd like to know others experiences We also have auxiliary headlights that are actually far more polite than factory, made frome gutted f150 halogen projectors (so oem style won't blind people) and those have a cutoff so im worried they'll see any light over 40in and assume the better lights are bad

Yeah, factory halogen headlights aren't going to be an issue. The hard cutoff is more about projector lights, aftermarket lights, and shitty LED lights. Just be sure to aim your F150 frankensteined lights so that the projector cutoff meets the spec, and you should be fine. You'll be required to turn on absolutely all of your forward-facing lights during the test, so plan accordingly. Jeff

Yeah kinda what I figured, but im wondering like how they do the test. Like is it just a board at 25 ft with a line at 40 in and of there's light above the line you fail or is it more seat of their pants judgement call. Im sure we'll pass on a judgment call but not sure we meet the exact letter of the rules. Like I said the aftermarket lights are the most polite and well within the rules. The oem lights aren't blinding either they just leak a little light high

It is indeed a board with a line on it. For the overnight races the cars are tested one-by-one the evening before the race and those which pass are given a distinctive windshield sticker (separate from the regular tech sticker) that allows them to be taken on track during "headlight hours" which are more or less from around dusk to around dawn, but which will be specifically announced at the event by race control. Without that sticker the car can still be raced during daylight hours, but that would mean missing about half the race. As Teuobk indicated, factory headlights in their stock location and with proper aim aren't going to be a problem. The judges are well aware that older headlight styles don't have well-defined cutoffs like newer units but they will still check that they aren't aimed unusually high. By the way, just in case anyone is thinking of getting the sticker and then changing their lighting to something that wouldn't pass, a car can (and will) still be turned back at the track entrance or black-flagged on track for lighting issues even with a sticker, so please don't.

title: All the farkles...
text:
Q: Can I add theme decorations like fake bull heads or large wings to my car, and are there size or location limits?
A: Theme items are completely budget-exempt and don't count toward the $500 cost limit. However, Rule 3.10.6 prohibits structural elements that extend past the original bodywork line (like nerf bars or crash bars). For theme pieces, the practical guidance is: secure them so they absolutely cannot fall off during racing (using frangible materials, metal brackets, and tension wires if needed), and avoid creating hazards that could damage other cars or red-flag the course. If your theme item can fit in a trailer and is rock-solid, you're probably fine—just don't be the person whose decoration flies off on track.
author: thunderchicken
original_text_truncated:
So, for a car to be eligible it needs to meet the $500 rule (exempt for safety gear etc.) What about all the ridiculous crap we put into our car theme? If I mount a fake bull head to the roof, do I need to have a receipt for said bull head? I hope the farkles are exempt, but I can't seem to find any exemption for "non-performance, non-reliability" items; am I just missing it with my man-eyes? Thanks

all theme stuff is absolutely budget exempt. a good rule of thumb is that the chassis, engine, drivetrain and suspension bits need to be $500 or less.

And even then, if part of your theme is "terrible engine swap" where you put a jaguar V12 in a renault 4cv... have at it.

As mentioned none of that counts towards cost, just show up with something safe for the 1st race, focus on getting it past tech and being able to run laps...worry about a theme after you finish those things.

Ok, followup question. Is there an easy rule of thumb about how big theme items can be/ where they can be located? I've been working on a stupid "wing" for our hooptie, but it's currently 6ft wide... Are we allowed to overhang the bodywork? Should we be building up instead of out? I'm thinking of a rough "legality box" so I don't build glorious crap that I need to strip off to race... Thanks

I think no, there was a 12 ft tall pirate ship, a stair car etc. However my opinion is try to avoid risk of stuff coming off and causing flat tires and windshield damage etc. It would suck to ruin someone's race they may not have spares. So wood and sheet metal screws are lame imo Also when theme explodes and red flags the course for 30 minutes it's kinda lame The judges probably say bigger is better.

This is different: "3.10.6 Nerf Bars Not Allowed. Added structural elements that extend past the original bodywork line are not allowed. In other words, no nerf bars, wheel-protection cages, or crash bars. (Worried about your car being damaged? Here’s an idea. Don’t hit anyone.)" but admittedly doesn't discuss what you're asking about, as the rule appears to be concerned with drivers being somewhat less concerned about contact if their vehicle is armored. I'll be interested to see what others chime in with on this subject; but I think the last half of your last sentence (above) is what will be your future. It doesn't read as if you're considering carrying a camper trailer over your vehicle.

There was a car at Road Atlanta a couple of years ago with a structural Snoopy doghouse on top. It fell off in like 4 seconds on track. Don't be that person. Practically, if it fits in the trailer and is secured so it doesn't fall off on track, it's probably fine.

Pagel's answer when we mounted a 1:5 merkury space capsule to our roof was: "Either make it out of frangible materials, or make sure to hell that it will never come off" So we kind of did both. MDF wrapped in vinyl with metal eye bolts and tension wires, and the base cross-bolted through metal L brackets so even if the guide wires failed the entire thing would still stay in place. It worked great until our roof got a little smashed and wouldn't hold tension anymore, so we don't run it out of abundance of caution.

Glad we have an open car trailer, we're halfway there!

citation: Forum: Adjustable Seat Question + Ground Clearance
title: Adjustable Seat Question + Ground Clearance
text:
Q: Can we drill holes in a fiberglass racing seat to install a seat brace, and what's the minimum ground clearance we need?
A: You cannot drill holes in a fiberglass (GRP) seat, but you can drill into an aluminum seat or use a separate metal brace (like a T-shaped tube or large plate) that doesn't require holes in the seat itself. For ground clearance, your vehicle must be high enough to get on and off a trailer safely and must not allow the chassis or exhaust to rest on the ground even with a flat tire—generally no lower than the wheel rim without a mounted tire.
author: CPlatt5
original_text_truncated:
New here... all the help is appreciated!! We are going to have a wide variety of drivers and personal preferences on seat adjustments in Datsun Z. We 're planning to have a slider mechanism buried in a dropped seat pan along with an adjustable seat brace to lock position quickly. My question is...we have a Sparco seat and putting any holes into a GRP seat is not allowed to add in the seat brace. It looks like adding holes into an aluminum seat is OK. Any suggestions or ideas? One last question...our fabricator dropped the seat pan to make sure we have the necessary helmet clearance... is there any specific rule for ground clearance? I think we're 3-4" and have only bottomed out of driveways so far. Thanks, Clay

In terms of vehicle ground clearance, think about the required clearance for getting it on and off a trailer. Second, ride height needs to be sufficient to not allow the chassis or exhaust to rest on the ground with flat tire(s). Generally, no lower than the wheel rim (without a mounted tire).

You don't have to add holes to a non-metal seat to add a brace. My brace is made of T shape tubing the width of the seat, others have large metal plates to distribute the load. Mount your seat sliders at an angle so when you move the seat forward it also goes up for shorter drivers.

citation: Forum: Fire Suppression Recertification Date - Acceptable
title: Fire Suppression Recertification Date - Acceptable?
text:
Q: How do I know if my fire suppression bottle's recertification is still valid for racing?
A: Fire suppression bottles must be recertified every 2 years. Check the recertification sticker on your bottle to find the expiration date—if it shows a month and year, your bottle is good through the last day of that month in the year shown. If you're unsure about reading the sticker or whether your bottle is current, take a clear photo of it and email it to Pagel at pagel@24hoursoflemons.com for official confirmation. Keep any email confirmation as proof in case a tech inspector questions it at the track.
author: Hoffshaw/Hinmann
original_text_truncated:
I have read that bottles require recertification every 2 years. My bottle, purchased new in 2020, has a recertification sticker with "24" year and "July" month chads removed. When is the exact last month/year this bottle is acceptable for racing?

That would mean that your bottle expired cert on the last day of July in 2024 and has not been acceptable for almost a year now.

Hmmm. Ok, we've ran twice this year so far... I rechecked the sticker and it states "recert." at the top, so now thinking we got it recertified July '24 and guessing that means we are good through July 2026, correct?

Take a (clear) picture of the sticker and and email it to Pagel ( pagel@24hoursoflemons.com ) and see what he says.... If he says you're 'good to go',save that email as your proof...

Do you not remember getting your fire bottle recertified? Advice to take a picture and send it to Pagel is the best advice. Sometimes the tech inspectors miss stickers, but that's not something you can (or should) count on.

Ok, thx.

citation: Forum: Set off airbags then remove?
title: Set off airbags then remove?
text:
Q: Can I safely remove airbags from my car for interior stripping, and what's the proper procedure?
A: Yes, you can remove airbags safely by disconnecting the battery first and letting it sit for 30 minutes to discharge residual power, then unplugging the airbag connectors before removal. Some teams sell removed airbags to recoup costs. If you're concerned about accidental deployment during removal, you can intentionally fire them outside the car using long wires connected to a 12V battery while standing far away, though this creates a mess of powder and is optional—most teams simply disconnect and remove them carefully without firing.
author: bcode
original_text_truncated:
I was thinking if I am stripping the interior anyway one of my concerns is setting off an airbag by accident. So if I can set them off while not being inside the car and not having to hit any crash structures to set them off how might one go about it? `09 VW GTI Was hoping to hear my idea is genius and here is how you might accomplish that safely. But feel free to call me an idiot and I can forget about this idea and move on.

Normally you can remove them without setting them off. Disconnect the battery a day or two before to let any residual electrons evaporate. If you can reach them, unplug them first. Not really possible for the steering wheel...

Should be able to sell them and recoup some cost.

instead of selling them, just rig up some looooooong wires to a 12v battery and launch a toilet or two!

There are grounding bars to stop you blowing them up with static shock, need the connector plugged in, so I cut the wires and plug the connector back in I recommend blowing them with long wires standing far away. I'd avoid doing it in the car as they tend to shoot some powder and make a mess. Should be 30min of battery disconnected to start airbag work, although many of us ignore that (at your own risk)

I'd remove it and sell it. Along with any other sensors.

title: Main hoop question
text:
Q: Can I bend my main hoop so it's narrower at the base than at the shoulders, even if it exceeds 180 degrees total?
A: Lemons doesn't explicitly restrict main hoop bend angles in its current rules, but the standard guideline from other series is 180 degrees ± 10 degrees total. If your bends fall within that ±10 degree tolerance, you should be fine at tech. The safest approach is to email Lemons tech directly (John Pagel is the authority) before building, and make sure your main hoop follows the "B" pillars in width as closely as possible while staying as close to the roof in height as you can.
author: FireChicken
original_text_truncated:
Hey y'all. I have a question about bending a main hoop to be narrower at the base than the shoulders. I want to ask here first before bothering the big guys directly. I know some other race rules call out main hoop bends not exceeding 180 degrees total but not Lemons specifically. Will Lemons tech call this out at all or give it side-eye even though rules don't mention it? I've bent two main hoops up for my 4th gen Firebird to compare and the overbent hoop fits much nicer with a better envelope than the one that is 180 total. This is only a couple of degrees over on each side. Anyone else running like this? All ears over here. Thanks for any help!

This is why your first email/call should be to Lemons tech, and not the forums. Do you never plan to race in another series for the duration of the car's life as a race car? Seems kinda not great to limit yourself at this stage... In terms of safety, I believe the 180 guideline is the most safe, if you exceed that and the car is on the roof, doesn't that extra rounding increase the danger of cage collapse? Pictures help.

Our cage is widest in the middle (near the harness bar iirc) So the top bends are less than 90, and then there is another bend that pushes the feet of the hoop inwards a little. I think the bottom is still wider than the top, but total bend angles are greater than 180. Didn't get a blink at tech, no problems (of course email to be sure)

Zacks, thanks for sharing your first hand experience. My hoop is like yours from your description. Mike, thanks for the reply. It's a 25 year old v6 firebird, Lemons is probably going to be it's only home. I dug into things a bit further and both of the instances where I saw this also mention a +/- 10 degree variance which my hoop would fall within. I was skimming the first time and didn't catch it before I let my paranoia get the better of me.

We run a Gen III firebird so I'm not sure I can help, Glue Sticks have run a Gen IV forever, message them and see how they installed their cage.

Thanks Yuck! I will see about contacting them. Loose Nuts Racing also runs a Gen 4 Camaro but my attempts to get in touch with them have failed; I did manage to get some screen caps of their build pics though and have been kinda loosely following them. I would love to have someone in a gen 4 to bounce ideas off of.

Hey - thanks for your link (to the source of your concern) via email. Short answer is: always contact John Pagel; he's always helpful (and authoritative), but also very busy. I've done just a little checking, though I'm not an authority . Bottom line, currently: follow the "B" pillars (citations below). DETAIL: I saw your concern listed in one location; but it's older, and has been superceded by more current rules which apply to new builds. It may still apply to others, but not to you or me. SCCA and 'the other series' have similar text in their respective current rules; 'the other series' being more straightforward because of fewer appendices: ////// [NOT Lemons RULE] 'the other series' 2025 RULES 2025 BCCR v1.0.1 [snip] 3.2.1.2. On all closed cars, the main hoop must be as close as possible to the roof (in height) and “B” pillars (in width) ////// SCCA 2025 GENERAL COMPETITION RULES Updated Through Technical Bulletin 25-05 9.4. ROLL CAGES FOR GT AND PRODUCTION BASED CARS All cars must utilize a roll cage compliant with the following specifications. These specifications apply to all vehicles registered (issued an SCCA logbook) after 1/1/08. [snip] [NOT Lemons RULE] B. MAIN HOOP 1. The main hoop (behind the driver) must be the full width of the cockpit for all cars. It must be one continuous length of tubing with smooth bends and no evidence of crimping or wall failure. The main hoop must maintain a single plane. a. On all closed cars, the main hoop must be as close as possible to the roof and “B” pillars. /////// SCCA APPENDIX I. 2007 ROLL CAGE RULES This appendix is present so that cages in cars with logbooks from 2007 and earlie r c

title: Fueling equipment
text:
Q: Can we wear fuel-resistant gloves over our Nomex driving gloves while fueling to keep our good gloves clean?
A: No—you can't wear anything over your racing gear unless that item itself is properly SFI or FIA rated. Fuel-resistant gloves like nitrile or rubber aren't fire-resistant and could melt to your hands in a fire, defeating the purpose of your safety gear. The best solution is to buy a dedicated, budget-friendly pair of SFI-rated Nomex gloves specifically for fueling duties, or rotate old driving gloves into the fueling role and let fuel evaporate before they're worn again.
author: ssaxer
original_text_truncated:
Can fuel resistant gloves be worn over the standard Nomex gloves for fueling so as to avoid getting fuel on your driving gloves?

We need the powers that be to answer, but this is a great idea.

I use my old driving gloves for fueling.

This is the way...... Surely you or one of your teammates has an old pair laying around...

We are a new team so all of our gear is new.

I would love to know what the powers to be have to say about this, often I have been the driver getting out of the car after my stint and I am the fuel can man so my drivers gloves get used I just ordered new glovers and the old ones have only been used for 6 races

Fuel resistant gloves aren't necessarily fire resistant. Fire is the reason you are wearing full safety gear with your visor down. So my guess is no, you wouldn't want nitrile or rubberized gloves melting to your hands even if you had nomex under it. Best thing is to find a budget set of SFI nomex gloves that fits everyone on your team and make that pair the "dedicated fueling gloves".

What kind of jugs are you using, race jugs should spill very little when used correctly. DOT approved gas cans seem to spill a ton.

Nope. That's pretty much why. You can't wear anything over your racing gear unless that item is itself appropriately SFI or FIA rated. No rain gear while fueling, for example. That works although it is of course "one more thing" for someone new to acquire. It's also a good idea to plan things so that, whenever possible, the person about to get into the car isn't the person handling the fuel jugs.

I bought another pair the team uses for fueling

The simple answer for us is "driver getting out pours gas" at all refuel stops. In the event of a spill, his/her gloves have almost 2 hours for fuel to evaporate before the next time they are put on. And a couple of us have spare gloves that others can borrow if needed, just in case the driver's gloves got drenched rather than just a small splash.

Sound reasoning, but I gotta say, when I’ve done 2 hours in the car I am searching for a nice bottle of water, not in a great frame of mind to handle a fuel jug, usually. The whole fueling rigamarole has led our team to use the track pumps and stay off the hot pit. Might be the better strategy until your team is such a well-oiled machine that a class win is a strong possibility. (To clarify, I’m talking about the OP’s team. Your crew seems to have figured this stuff out, Stan

I would suggest that the driver getting out of the car man’s the fire bottle. The next driver hands off cans. The guy fueling wears the gas gloves and is just the gas man for that stop. The reality is, too many people are trying to do the stop as fast as possible. Slow down, be deliberate, not fast. If it takes you a minute extra per stop to not spill fuel and increase your chance of catching on fire, it’s worth it.

This is how we do it.

There was a previous discussion about this subject that had quite a bit more about the exiting driver's condition after a stint, and whether that's the best team member to have pouring fuel; however, this thread is as succinct as I've seen this subject, so this is now my reference point. I'm going with Tim and rb, and: 'team fueling gloves', exiting driver has the fire bottle - or nothing, and new driver doesn't get fuel on them ... assuming team's numbers support that. My perspective is the 'older, in experienced guy'; I would not be looking forward to pouring fuel after a stint. My perspective is also that of pouring from regular commercial red gas cans into restricted filter neck openings through funnels which neck down to about 1/4" openings, not vented quick-empty cans into racing tanks. But congratulations to Stan for having (by reputation, above) a team experienced and capable enough, with enough equipment (I'm assuming) to do that. That's flat out impressive.

Take a hole saw to the inside of the filler neck to remove the unleaded restrictor, and get some Hunsackers or VP style jugs

citation: Forum: Honda oil pan gasket leak help
title: Honda oil pan gasket leak help
text:
Q: My Honda oil pan gasket keeps leaking even after multiple replacements and proper torquing—what's going wrong?
A: The issue is likely a combination of factors: the aftermarket oil pan may have misaligned or missing locating pimples that hold the gasket in place, the vent hole to the PCV system may be partially blocked, and excessive oil pressure buildup in the pan during racing can force the gasket out. Clean all mating surfaces thoroughly, use OEM gaskets if possible (or high-quality alternatives like Hondabond/Toyotaseal RTV), torque conservatively to the lower end of the spec, and consider using gasket adhesive to keep the gasket positioned during assembly. If the aftermarket pan is damaged or misaligned, you may need to source a better-quality pan or OEM unit.
author: MZAVARIN
original_text_truncated:
1987 Honda Prelude It can't be that difficult...! Oil was leaking out of engine (valve cover breather, catch can, etc.) over several races last years and getting worse, so we took the engine apart, and it turned out that the piston rings were broken....and the tops of the pistons!....amazing that we actually raced with this problem and did fine...and the engine block cylinders were absolutely fine!! Like new! we put the engine back together, including the oil pan, which specified 10 ft-lb torque....we did not have a small enough torque wrench, so we just did it snugly...and I took it home and used my Harbor Freight battery torque wrench that goes down to 6 ft-lb and snugged whatever bolts I could get to to 10 ft-lb .....about half of them.... Car went out on bracket race and leaked oil...a fair amount....around perimeter of bulging oil pan gasket....(used goop to keep racing) Gasket replaced and all bolts tightened to 10 ft-lbs....leaked again Gasket replaced again and bolts tightened to 7 or 8 ft- lbs now with rented torque wrench (84 in-lbs) ....(lesson learned: torque wrenches are not very accurate at both ends of their range....!) and used gasket sealant adhesive But leaked again....! Gasket used is standard RockAuto stuff Engine is mostly stock....we did do head gasket change last year and shaved 1.2 mm off the head....and some other stuff (3 angle valve job, etc.) Oil pan looks fine, not warped or damaged. Oil pan has tube going to a condenser ? can....then to PCV valve....then to our catch can....then to intake vacuum... Oil pressure gauge does not work... Obviously oil filter new And applied some silicone sealant to corners, as specified, and that is not where it was leaking I have oil cooler installed I can clearly see where oil is coming from at points where oil gasket bulges out.... ?? More torque, less torque, or is there some kind of pressure build up in the oil pan that is making the gasket bulge out??? thats all the details I remember thoughts, suggestions, ideas, experiences, condolences??? Thanks! mz

I was going to say maybe a bend rail or something. Are you 100% sure it's the pan? Unless there's a ton of pressure the pan should not be leaking too badly. My only other thought is the block. Is something on the block side not right??? The engine needs to be ventilated. We run a SBC and for the 1st race or 2 is was peeing oil out of the PCV so much so we installed a breather on one side and a puke tank on the other, with a vent. No issues since. Remember these engines were designed for little bouts of performance and mostly light duty, so running the piss out of it for 4 hours is going to build up pressure. I'm not a Honda guy but it sounds like you may have a pressure issue.

i've never had good luck with anything other than OEM, from the honda dealer oil pan gaskets. seems like they're made of sturdier stuff than what you get from rockauto. i'd strongly recommend starting there and be SURE follow the shop manual tightening sequence.

Thanks Yuck! The block looked fine and I cleaned it well where the gasket attaches. I've been running the car, engine for many years, many races and never had this problem. Seems like the pressure would be relieved via PCV, to catch can, to intake....although I used to have a problem where the dip stick would pop out partially and oil would spew out the tube....so now I have a small spring holding the dip stick in...! Thanks incomplete- I did follow the shop manual tightening sequence.... and unfortunately not much OEM is available for this old car, including oil pan gasket...even junk yard parts are getting hard to find....I can't imagine what people do with cars older than mine... A donor car I stripped several years ago and filled my shed with spare parts has been a big savior over the years....! I wonder: maybe just do a road draft tube? bypass PVC, to catch can to collect oil, to atmosphere....

Can you just rtv that sucker? I've had luck with the transmission rtv which is us

citation: Forum: Can I cut this part off the roof of my car?
title: Can I cut this part off the roof of my car?
text:
Q: Can I remove the bumpers from my car to save weight?
A: You can remove bumpers entirely—many Lemons cars run without them. However, experienced teams recommend keeping them for crash protection, as they can be the difference between a disabled car and one that can limp back to the pits. If weight is your concern, try removing just the bumper mounting hardware or finding lighter aluminum bumpers from similar-era vehicles instead.
author: wvumtnbkr
original_text_truncated:
We race a 78 chevy malibu 2 door. Can I cut off the roof behind the main hoop? There is an "A" , "B", and "C" pillar on the car. I want to cut off the "C" pillar and about 1 foot of roof. The "B" pillar would still be on the car and the roof would end right behind it. Essentially it is doing nothing there. I also want to take off the bumpers. They are enormously heavy steel things. Can I do this and not replace them with anything? Thanks! Rob R.

The roof: Yes. The bumper: No. Rules state no modified bumpers, safety concerns yo.

I think taking them off is fine, beefing them up is the no no.

I thought I could take them off. I see tons of Lemons cars with no bumpers.

If this car is a potential long-term investment, consider leaving the bumpers, despite the weight. They can be the difference between a disabled car and a happy but ugly car.

Or go out and find a ford fairmont from the same era. Similar big bumpers, but aluminum.

I'll tack on my question here, seems like a good spot. I don't have a front bumper for the Biturbo. Didn't come with the car. Am I allowed to install something that fits within the original body /trim dimensions? Like a piece of hollow rectangular medium gauge steel box type stuff? The idea is to gain similar protection to factory specs but not spend $$$ trying to buy an original bumper.

When in doubt, email John Pagel or Jeff Glenn and ask them what they think.

I agree even if you can take them off leave them, the race is about staying on track and having the bumpers esp. with that car has more protection. we hit the wall and had a bunch of damage but with out the bumper the gar would have been at the scrap yard

I'd leave the bumpers, you could get rid of some of the mounts, that's were the weight is. Also consider gutting the hood and trunk inner structure, basically making them sheet metal, the inner door structure is also very heavy. Lots of weight can be taken off of these cars, we run a Gen III F-body. If you do take the bumpers off, I'd fabricate some sort of bumper, like a bent piece of 1/4" steel that's 4" and goes from headlight to headlight.

Zombie thread alert!

citation: Forum: Front strut replacement in case of one leaking - w
title: Front strut replacement in case of one leaking - wwyd - both?
text:
Q: If one strut starts leaking during a race due to impact damage, should I replace both struts or just the one?
A: At the track, do the bare minimum to keep racing—replace just the leaking strut if you have enough time left in the event to make up the lost performance. A single leaking strut typically costs you a few seconds per lap, so unless you're down to the final hour or two, it's usually not worth the pit time to replace both. However, make sure your replacement strut matches the brand and part number of the other one. Before the race, you'll have time to do both properly, which is the right call for a car you'll be running multiple events with.
author: Lemon_Newton-Metre
original_text_truncated:
Sure, normal procedure for me on a daily would be to replace both at the same time. Brakes, calipers, ball joints, all that stuff, sure. But: if you had only one leaking because of hitting curbing or a pothole, and they were both relatively young (as compared to the car), would you replace both automatically? I mean like during a race if you had 'used, good' spares. Or would you run until the track went cold? I'm not going to make NJMP, and I'll have plenty of time to do both before this is ready. I'm trying to get an idea of what people expect of 'racetime' repairs.

Depending on the vehicle could be a long hateful job. I'd say if they are new, I'd just get the one, but make sure it is the same brand and part number as the other.

On race car if due to impact would only do 1. At race would do bare minimum to stay on track, would not replace strut for leak during race. Let's say whatever track you are at you run 2 min laps avg. let's say the blown strut costs you 5 sec a lap let's say you have 5 hrs left so you'd lose about 12 min or 6 laps. Can you change a strut in 12 min? Obviously those are made up numbers but 5 sec a lap is a lot to lose for 1 strut leaking even at double still not worth it. If there was only an hr or 2 left?? Absolutely not. That being said last race we made the mistake of not replacing a wheel bearing from going off track, ended up coming in to check it, driving slower due to scary noises and then replacing an hour later anyway. Should have sucked it up and made the repair first time car was in. But that's a more direct safety item than a strut.

citation: Forum: Baby's First Lemons Car - 85 Camaro Z28
title: Baby's First Lemons Car - 85 Camaro Z28
text:
Q: What's the best way to build a roll cage for my 3rd-gen Camaro, and can I weld it myself with a small flux-core welder?
A: Don't weld the cage yourself with a flux-core welder—Lemons tech inspectors reject flux-core cages because the welds don't penetrate properly and the spatter creates weak joints. Pre-bent cage kits from reputable builders like Roll Cage Components or Kirk Racing Products (in Alabama) are your best bet; they require professional MIG welding with a 220V-capable welder and must be fully welded to the floor and body, not bolted. Get cage recommendations from experienced Lemons racers in your region before choosing a builder, since some shops cut corners on Lemons-specific requirements.
author: cwyckisslick94
original_text_truncated:
I've been aware of 24hrs of Lemons for about a decade, but haven't ever really been in a position to commit the time and resources into participation. I have an idiot friend who has made it very easy to obtain a car, and potentially get black flags in the future; this is right up his alley too. Said friend has a '86 Camaro RS, 305 700r4, transmission went out, he decides to rebuild it and can't get it to work, rebuilds it about 3-4 more times. Buys a junkyard transmission, rebuilds that another 3-4 times. The last time the transmission goes in the car, the 305 is puking out the rear main, bound in reverse, only has 1st. This guy goes out and buys a running, driving, '85 Camaro Z28 that's been 350 swapped, drives it home, and parts it out for his car. In the process he "rebuilds" the 350, loses a rod bolt, puts a random bolt through the rod cap, and it comes apart 3 miles down the road. From 5 minutes of driving he's decided a 350 isn't enough so he's looking for a 400. He thought no one wanted the '85 so it's in a million pieces, it legitimately baffled him that I wanted this car, and he gave it to me, and all of the goodies he doesn't want to mess with anymore, for free. As far as I know, I get the leaky 305, the blown up 350, the TPI intake, both junk 700r4's, and all the parts to put humpty back together again. This has been a developing situation for some time but I waited until I had a title to post here. I just put the axle back in the car so I can haul it off to my secret lair later this week. I imagine the 305 TPI can go back in it. I picked up two transmissions from a local junkyard for $25 a piece almost a year ago, one of them happens to be a 9" tail TH350 that will fit the stock driveshaft, allegedly. There's a DOM prebuilt cage that appears to be Lemons legal made by Rhodes, I'll leave that to viewer's discretion; The car also needs a windshield and a floor. 3rd gens are pretty hard to find, I have already considered cutting a 4th gen firewall and floor out and have the entire front subframe swapped, apparently this has been done before but I couldn't find a photo. I'll look more at it in the coming weeks. We hope to start at NCM '25 as that's local here In Bowling Green and I've been to the event a couple times. I'm curious what other 3rd gen drivers have done to their cars, videos of Lemons camaros on youtube tend to look pretty cheaty and fast.

I bend my own cages, but by all accounts the Rhodes cages are made for cars with full interior. This makes them less than likely to pass tech. They also reportedly run an oddball tube size. YMMV. YOLO. TMI. YAATMN. Not a pro, not a customer, not a Lemons official, just a guy on the internet. No statements are legally binding. Establish a safeword that is easy to pronounce through a ball gag.

I see, our second option is to find someone locally who can make the cage. I've only got a titanium 125 flux core and a couple years of using it on the farm.

Where are you (state is probably close enough for someone "local" to chime in)?

Kentucky/Tennessee

There should be lots of options depending on what you consider local and a thriving Lemons community to chime in. Get recommendations from Lemons peoples before you go to a shop, because there is at least one horror-show a year where somebody bought a cage from a builder who didn't follow the rules because they "know better than that fool Tech Tyrant." I have heard good things about the kits that Roll Cage Components sells, but again, I can't really tell you anything about how they come or how much work they are to install. I can say that installing a cage is an order of operations problem if you want it welded right, and the order is not necessarily intuitive. Like, you can't just tack the whole thing in place and call a mobile welder unless you want random holes cut in the bodywork to access 360 degrees of the joint, or fail tech for not being 360 and then some dude in the paddock cuts random holes in the bodywork to finish
